Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed declarative clause; noun + 1SG pronoun + stative verb compound.
Figurative Meaning: To carry hidden resentment or silent anger.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from traditional cooking stones (sogo) that retain heat long after fire fades.
First Example Sentence: Tumo luma na sogo-beli, amai nabe foledo.
Second Example Sentence: Na luma na sogo-beli, tumo meli gesele.
Third Example Sentence: Amai luma na sogo-beli fo, mubi tale.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in hunting metaphors where animals are lured away from paths.
First Example Sentence: Na amai tumo fele-dabo, tumo nabe meli.
Second Example Sentence: Tumo amai fele-dabo fo, soro bile.
Third Example Sentence: Amai tumo fele-dabo labe, na kadele.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Refers to rivers (soro) that overflow unpredictably during heavy rain.
First Example Sentence: Na soro na luma-feda fo, tumo sile.
Third Example Sentence: Tumo soro na luma-feda fo labe.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Compound verb phrase in perfective aspect.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Associated with ceremonial masks hiding facial expression.
Second Example Sentence: Tumo mubi lale-fole fo tumo nabe.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause with possessive noun phrase.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Linked to traditional shell trumpets (mubi) blown loudly in public gatherings.
First Example Sentence: Na tale na mubi-safa fo, tumo bile.
Second Example Sentence: Amai tale na mubi-safa fo labe.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Refers to cooling embers after communal cooking.
Inspired by ritual face painting concealing emotion.
Based on carrying heavy garden loads together.
Evokes crossing deep rivers during tribal migrations.
Derived from imagery of smoke drifting away from hearth fire.
